tool_call_sequence.description: Accurately recognizing cluster headache (familial history supportive but not required) is the primary outcome for an L1 diagnosis task and guides appropriate acute and preventive management discussions.

nl_assertions: Agent identifies the presentation as consistent with cluster headache (severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain, short-lasting attacks, occurring in clusters) and does not diagnose migraine or sinusitis as the primary condition when classic features are present.

Agent explicitly explains the diagnosis using the term "cluster headache" and links it to ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms (e.g., lacrimation, conjunctival injection, rhinorrhea/nasal congestion, eyelid edema, facial sweating, miosis/ptosis) and/or restlessness/agitation.

Agent asks about red flags and secondary causes (first/worst headache, new onset >50, fever/meningismus, focal neurologic deficits, altered mental status, thunderclap onset, head trauma, immunosuppression/cancer, pregnancy/postpartum, papilledema, painful red eye/vision loss) and indicates need for urgent evaluation if present.

Agent characterizes attack timing and pattern (duration 15–180 minutes untreated, frequency up to multiple/day, circadian/seasonal periodicity, nocturnal attacks) and laterality (strictly unilateral, side-locked) to support diagnostic confidence.

Agent elicits key associated features to distinguish from migraine (lack of prominent nausea/photophobia/phonophobia or presence but less prominent; prominent autonomic signs and restlessness) and from trigeminal neuralgia (brief electric shocks) and paroxysmal hemicrania (shorter, more frequent, indomethacin-responsive).
Agent acknowledges familial occurrence can increase likelihood and briefly notes that cluster headache can have a genetic component without overclaiming inheritance certainty.

Agent explains evidence-based acute treatments (high-flow oxygen and a triptan) as part of counseling, even though prescribing is not required at L1, and avoids recommending opioids as first-line therapy.

If the agent discusses treatment options, it includes safety framing: check allergies, contraindications, and drug interactions before prescribing triptans (e.g., coronary/cerebrovascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension, hemiplegic/basilar migraine, recent ergot use, MAOI interactions depending on triptan) and advises caution with smoking/vascular risk assessment.
Agent provides clear return precautions (seek urgent care for atypical features, neurologic deficits, persistent visual symptoms, severe eye pain/redness, or sudden thunderclap headache).
Communication is patient-centered, uses understandable language, and confirms understanding (teach-back or invitation for questions).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose cluster headache based on classic unilateral severe attacks with ipsilateral autonomic symptoms and cluster periodicity; provide brief explanation and safety screening for red flags.
steps: Elicit headache characteristics: location, severity, duration, frequency, timing (night/circadian), side-locked nature.
Ask about ipsilateral autonomic symptoms and restlessness during attacks.
Screen for red flags/secondary headache features and determine if urgent evaluation is needed.
Differentiate briefly from migraine, sinusitis, trigeminal neuralgia, paroxysmal hemicrania.
Explain diagnosis using the terms "cluster headache" and "autonomic symptoms" and outline that attacks occur in clusters.
Mention first-line acute options (oxygen and triptan) at a high level and advise that safety checks (allergy/contraindications/interactions) are required before prescribing.
Document diagnosis via tool.

acceptable_variants.description: Probable cluster headache when most features fit but one element is uncertain (e.g., duration not clearly established), with plan to confirm pattern and evaluate for secondary causes if atypical.
steps: Collect core features and autonomic symptoms; note any uncertainty (e.g., unclear duration/frequency).
Screen for red flags and consider need for neuroimaging if atypical/new onset or abnormal exam.
Explain likely diagnosis and what additional history will confirm it.
Discuss acute options (oxygen, triptan) conceptually and safety checks before prescribing.
Record diagnosis as cluster headache vs probable cluster headache.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis with explicit differential documentation in narrative (without using the record_differential tool, since L1 outcome is diagnosis-only) and clear reasoning for ruling out common mimics.
steps: Elicit and document distinguishing features (autonomic signs, restlessness, short duration, cluster pattern).
Ask targeted questions to rule out sinusitis (purulent discharge/fever), glaucoma (vision loss/red eye), temporal arteritis (>50, jaw claudication), dissection (neck pain, neuro deficits).
Explain cluster headache and autonomic symptoms; provide return precautions.
Record diagnosis.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of MONA to enable appropriate counseling, genetic evaluation, and supportive management planning.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as “multicentric osteolysis, nodulosis, and arthropathy (MONA)” (or equivalent wording) and avoids incorrect labeling as inflammatory arthritis (e.g., JIA/RA) without justification.
Explains that MONA is a rare genetic disorder and explicitly mentions genetic testing as part of confirming/characterizing the condition (e.g., MMP2-related testing) and/or family counseling.
Explains treatment/supportive care and explicitly includes NSAIDs and physical therapy as key components of symptom management.
Communicates that management is primarily supportive (pain control, function/rehab, monitoring) and sets expectations about chronicity and progression in clear, patient-friendly language.

Performs required safety checks before any prescribing: asks about medication allergies before recommending/prescribing NSAIDs (critical) and screens for contraindications to NSAIDs (critical; e.g., kidney disease, GI ulcer/bleeding history, anticoagulant use, pregnancy considerations as applicable).
If discussing medication options, avoids unsafe/contraindicated NSAID use (e.g., in significant CKD, active GI bleeding) and offers safer alternatives or specialist input when indicated.
If medication is proposed, considers noncritical drug-interaction screening (e.g., anticoagulants, antiplatelets, SSRIs, ACEi/ARB+diuretic ‘triple whammy’) and documents/communicates the rationale.

Uses shared decision-making, checks understanding, and provides return precautions (e.g., worsening pain/swelling, functional decline, red-flag symptoms such as fever/weight loss suggesting alternate diagnoses).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose MONA based on characteristic osteolysis/arthropathy pattern and nodulosis; counsel on genetic nature and supportive management (NSAIDs + PT).
must_collect: subcutaneous nodules (hands/feet/elbows) or history of nodulosis
absence of systemic inflammatory features (e.g., persistent fever) or alternative explanation
family history of similar skeletal issues/consanguinity
steps: Elicit targeted history focusing on onset, progression, distribution of joint/bone involvement, nodules, and functional impact.

Review/confirm prior imaging findings consistent with multicentric osteolysis (especially carpal/tarsal involvement) and arthropathy pattern; note lack of classic erosive inflammatory arthritis features if applicable.
Explain diagnosis as multicentric osteolysis, nodulosis, and arthropathy and that it is typically genetic; recommend genetic testing as part of confirmation and family counseling.

Discuss supportive treatment plan emphasizing NSAIDs for pain/inflammation (with safety screening) and physical therapy to maintain function and mobility; discuss activity modification and assistive devices as needed.
Document the diagnosis using the WRITE tool.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis-first with explicit differential discussion (but still records MONA as final diagnosis) and emphasizes referral planning verbally without using referral WRITE tool (L0 constraint).
steps: Collect core MONA features (childhood onset, carpal/tarsal osteolysis, nodules, progressive arthropathy, functional limitations, family history).
Briefly discuss key differentials (e.g., juvenile idiopathic arthritis, multicentric carpal-tarsal osteolysis, mucopolysaccharidoses) and why MONA is most consistent.
Explain MONA and genetic testing; address prognosis and supportive care.
Explain treatment including NSAIDs and physical therapy; include safety screening for NSAID allergies/contraindications before any prescribing recommendation.
Record MONA as the diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Patient-centered counseling variant with stronger education and monitoring plan while keeping the single required WRITE action (record_diagnosis).
steps: Gather symptoms and functional impact plus prior imaging summary consistent with multicentric osteolysis and nodulosis.
Explain MONA in plain language; explicitly mention genetic testing and implications for family members.
Discuss symptomatic management including NSAIDs and physical therapy; include safety checks prior to any medication recommendation.
Provide home/rehab advice (joint protection, pacing, assistive devices) and clear return precautions.
Record the diagnosis as MONA.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'trigeminal neuralgia' and explains it in plain language.

Screens for red flags/secondary causes and atypical features: sensory loss or numbness, persistent pain between attacks, bilateral symptoms, age <40, hearing/visual symptoms, weakness, ataxia, constitutional symptoms, history of MS or tumor.

Performs/requests an appropriate focused neuro exam expectation (cranial nerve sensory testing) and notes whether sensory deficits are present; recognizes that sensory deficits suggest secondary trigeminal neuropathy rather than classic TN.

Considers and records key differentials verbally (even if not using record_differential tool): dental pathology, temporomandibular disorder, cluster headache/SUNCT/SUNA, postherpetic neuralgia, migraine, sinusitis, giant cell arteritis (if older with systemic symptoms), trigeminal neuropathy from tumor/MS.

Communication milestone: explains first-line treatment conceptually and mentions 'carbamazepine' as a typical first-line option and the need for baseline/monitoring labs including 'CBC' (without necessarily prescribing in this L1 task).
